The DMCA also heightens the penalties for copyright infringement on the Internet.[2]

The DMCA contains a number of important limitations on the exclusive rights of copyright owners. Section 1201 allows users to circumvent technological measures that restrict access to copyrighted works if the circumvention is "necessary" to accomplish another exempt activity, such as making backup copies or using works in the classroom. The DMCA also allows users to circumvent technological measures if the measures are "not effective" in preventing or restricting access to works in the public domain or to works for which the user holds a valid license.[3]

Section 1201 of the DMCA also prohibits the manufacture, importation, or sale of any technology, product, or service that is primarily designed or produced for the purpose of circumventing a technological measure that effectively controls access to a work protected by copyright. The statute also prohibits the manufacture, importation, or sale of any technology, product, or service that is primarily designed or produced for the purpose of circumventing a technological measure that effectively protects a right of a copyright owner in a work in which the copyright owner has afforded controlled access, and has limited application or distribution to persons who authorize access by means of such a technological measure.[4]

Section 1201 of the DMCA also requires copyright owners to provide a means of obtaining access to works protected by technological measures, and prohibits the imposition of liability on Internet service providers (ISPs) for violations of the DMCA by their subscribers. The statute also requires the Librarian of Congress to establish a triennial rulemaking process to determine whether exemptions should be granted for classes of works or categories of persons who would be adversely affected by the application of the DMCA.[5]
